medications.indication_rationale: IV dextrose halts catabolism and switches the body back to an anabolic state, suppressing further lactate production — the definitive first step in a mitochondrial metabolic crisis, given even when blood glucose is normal. It is hospital-administered; the telehealth-correct action is to recognise the need and escalate to 000 so it can be started in the ambulance/ED.

For severe, uncompensated acidaemia buffering may be used as an ED-directed adjunct after blood-gas confirmation — a hospital decision, not a remote prescription. Recorded here as part of the correct in-hospital pathway.

medications.drug_name: Oral glucose gel or a sugary drink (immediate carer stopgap)
medications.indication_rationale: While waiting for the ambulance, if the patient can still swallow safely, a carer can give oral glucose gel or a sugary drink to begin suppressing catabolism — the one useful action available at home. It does not replace IV therapy and 000 must not be delayed for it.
contraindications_in_this_case: Reduced consciousness / drowsiness with confusion — aspiration risk; only give by mouth if he is alert enough to swallow safely and protect his airway, otherwise wait for paramedics

medications.indication_rationale: The regular oral supplement is maintenance therapy, was vomited and unabsorbed, and does nothing for the acute crisis. It must not be relied on or re-dosed as the treatment for this emergency.
medications.deprescribing_note: Hold oral maintenance supplements during the acute crisis; resume once stable and able to absorb oral intake.

medications.drug_name: Oral rehydration solution as definitive home management
medications.indication_rationale: Coaching home oral rehydration as the plan is a dangerous error of commission — it delays IV dextrose and fluids in an unstable, encephalopathic patient. Oral fluids cannot reverse an established lactic-acidosis crisis.
contraindications_in_this_case: Encephalopathy and haemodynamic instability — home oral rehydration is unsafe and inadequate

medications.drug_name: Antiemetic (e.g. ondansetron/metoclopramide) as the primary fix
medications.indication_rationale: Treating the vomiting alone and arranging later review misreads the emergency — the vomiting is the trigger, not the problem to manage remotely. Any antiemetic is an in-hospital adjunct, never a substitute for escalation.
medications.dose_route_frequency: not applicable — no dose is prescribed remotely; flag for in-person/ED clinician decision

allied_health_referrals.rationale: After stabilisation, a metabolic dietitian should review the emergency/sick-day regimen (carbohydrate cover during illness) with the metabolic team.
Complex multisystem mitochondrial disease benefits from coordinated metabolic, cardiology and allied-health follow-up and an up-to-date emergency plan.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Adopt a written 'sick-day rule': any illness that prevents eating or drinking for more than about 4 hours means going to the emergency department for IV fluids, without waiting for weakness to develop.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Early IV carbohydrate/fluids prevents a reversible catabolic state from becoming a fatal crisis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Always carry the mitochondrial emergency letter (specifying the need for IV dextrose) and keep it current; show it to any treating team.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: It ensures responders start the correct metabolic emergency treatment without delay.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: During any minor illness, avoid fasting — maintain regular carbohydrate intake and seek help early if intake fails.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Fasting is the catabolic trigger for decompensation in mitochondrial disease.

patient_education_points.point: Your cells' energy factories can't keep up, so when the stomach bug stopped you eating, your body broke down muscle for fuel and produced a flood of lactic acid — that is what is causing the deep breathing, weakness and confusion.
A normal blood sugar reading does NOT mean this is safe — the danger is acid building up in the blood, not the sugar level. Glucose is still given to switch off the muscle breakdown.
This needs an intravenous drip in hospital urgently — it cannot be fixed by drinking fluids at home.
In future, if any illness stops you eating or drinking for more than about 4 hours, go straight to the emergency department for IV fluids and carry your mitochondrial emergency letter.

follow_up_plan.interval: immediate emergency transfer now; then intensive-care/metabolic-team admission, with metabolic specialist and cardiology review after discharge
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: airway, breathing and circulation, lactate/acid-base clearance, neurological recovery and hydration; then review and update the sick-day/emergency plan
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any worsening of breathing or consciousness, collapse, chest pain or seizure before the ambulance arrives — recontact 000 immediately

must_include_items: Recognise a life-threatening acute metabolic emergency (not ordinary gastroenteritis or baseline weakness)
Call 000 for an emergency ambulance without delay
Do NOT advise home rest / oral rehydration as the management
Convey that urgent IV dextrose and fluids in hospital are needed
Offer a safe carer stopgap (sugary drink/glucose gel only if he can swallow safely) without delaying 000
acceptable_alternatives: Immediate ambulance transfer to ED is the required disposition; naming the specific metabolic mechanism is desirable but not required for a pass as long as escalation is correct
errors_of_omission: Dismissing the presentation as viral gastroenteritis or as 'just his usual weakness' (diagnostic overshadowing)
Being falsely reassured by the normal glucose or normal SpO2
Not recognising deep, rapid breathing as a sign of a metabolic emergency
errors_of_commission: Advising oral rehydration / 'push fluids and rest at home' as the plan
Prescribing an antiemetic and arranging routine later review
Advising self-transport to hospital despite instability and encephalopathy
Telling the patient to re-take his oral mito cocktail as the treatment
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognition of a metabolic emergency AND 000 escalation. Under-triage to GP, telehealth review, or home rehydration is an automatic critical failure regardless of other content.
